name: Feature Engineering
description: Techniques for transforming raw data into meaningful features that improve machine learning model performance
key_facts: Feature engineering includes normalization, encoding categorical variables, handling missing values, and creating derived features
One-hot encoding converts categorical variables into binary vectors; label encoding assigns integer values
Feature scaling methods include min-max normalization (0-1 range) and standardization (zero mean, unit variance)
Feature importance analysis identifies which features contribute most to model predictions
AWS Glue DataBrew provides visual data preparation with 250+ built-in transformations
common_misconceptions: All features should be included in the model (irrelevant features add noise and increase training time)
One-hot encoding is always better than label encoding (ordinal features should use label encoding to preserve order)

name: Data Pipelines for ML
description: Building automated, scalable data ingestion and transformation pipelines that prepare data for model training and inference
key_facts: SageMaker Processing jobs run data preprocessing scripts on managed compute clusters
AWS Glue ETL jobs transform data at scale using Spark-based processing
Amazon Kinesis Data Streams and Firehose handle real-time data ingestion for streaming ML pipelines
SageMaker Pipelines orchestrate end-to-end ML workflows including data processing steps
Data validation checks should be automated to catch schema drift and data quality issues
common_misconceptions: Data pipelines only need to run once (production ML requires continuous data processing for retraining and inference)
SageMaker can only process data stored in S3 (it supports multiple data sources via processing jobs and containers)

name: SageMaker Data Wrangler
description: Visual data preparation tool in SageMaker Studio for exploring, transforming, and analyzing data with low-code workflows
key_facts: Provides a visual interface for data exploration with built-in statistical summaries and visualizations
Supports importing data from S3, Athena, Redshift, Lake Formation, and other data sources
Offers 300+ built-in data transformations for encoding, imputation, scaling, and feature creation
Data flows can be exported to SageMaker Pipelines, Processing jobs, or Feature Store
Quick Model feature provides rapid model training to evaluate feature quality
common_misconceptions: Data Wrangler is only for small datasets (it can process large datasets by running transformations on distributed compute)
Data Wrangler replaces custom preprocessing code (it is for exploration and prototyping; production pipelines often need custom code)

name: SageMaker Feature Store
description: Centralized repository for storing, sharing, and managing ML features with both online (low-latency) and offline (batch) access patterns
key_facts: Online store provides single-digit millisecond feature retrieval for real-time inference
Offline store persists features in S3 in Parquet format for batch training and analysis
Feature groups define the schema and organize related features together
Supports feature versioning, data lineage tracking, and feature reuse across teams
Features can be ingested via PutRecord API (real-time) or batch ingestion from S3
common_misconceptions: Feature Store only works with SageMaker models (features can be used by any ML framework or application)
Online and offline stores are separate systems (they can be synchronized so the same features serve both use cases)

name: SageMaker Training Jobs
description: Running model training on managed infrastructure using SageMaker training jobs with configurable compute, storage, and networking
key_facts: Training jobs provision compute instances, download training data from S3, run the training algorithm, and upload model artifacts to S3
Supports both built-in algorithms and custom training containers (bring-your-own-container)
Spot training instances can reduce training costs by up to 90% with managed spot interruption handling
Hyperparameter tuning jobs (HPO) automatically search for optimal hyperparameters using Bayesian optimization
Training metrics are logged to CloudWatch for monitoring training progress
common_misconceptions: Training jobs require a specific ML framework (SageMaker supports any framework via custom Docker containers)
Spot interruptions always require restarting training from scratch (checkpointing enables resuming from the last saved state)

name: SageMaker Built-In Algorithms
description: Pre-built, optimized ML algorithms provided by SageMaker for common use cases including classification, regression, clustering, and NLP
key_facts: XGBoost: gradient boosted trees for tabular classification and regression, the most widely used built-in algorithm
Linear Learner: efficient linear model for large-scale classification and regression
BlazingText: optimized Word2Vec and text classification at scale
Image Classification and Object Detection: built-in computer vision algorithms
Built-in algorithms are optimized for distributed training and GPU utilization on SageMaker infrastructure
common_misconceptions: Built-in algorithms are only for simple problems (XGBoost and deep learning algorithms handle complex tasks at scale)
You must use built-in algorithms on SageMaker (custom frameworks like PyTorch and TensorFlow are fully supported)

name: SageMaker Autopilot
description: Automated machine learning (AutoML) service that automatically explores data, selects algorithms, tunes hyperparameters, and generates candidate models
key_facts: Automatically analyzes tabular data, selects feature preprocessing and algorithms, and tunes hyperparameters
Generates multiple candidate models ranked by a target metric (accuracy, F1, RMSE, etc.)
Provides full visibility into the generated code: feature engineering notebooks and model training notebooks
Supports ensemble mode (best accuracy) and HPO mode (single algorithm tuning)
Deploys the best model directly to a SageMaker endpoint
common_misconceptions: Autopilot is a black box with no explainability (it generates transparent, editable notebooks showing every step)
Autopilot only works with CSV data (it also supports Parquet format)

name: Distributed Training
description: Techniques for training ML models across multiple GPUs and instances to handle large models and datasets that exceed single-machine capacity
key_facts: Data parallelism splits the training dataset across multiple GPUs, each processing a subset with the full model
Model parallelism splits the model itself across GPUs when the model is too large for a single GPU
SageMaker Distributed Training Library provides optimized implementations of both data and model parallelism
Pipeline parallelism splits the model across stages, with micro-batches flowing through the pipeline
Efficient communication between GPUs (AllReduce, Ring-AllReduce) is critical for distributed training performance
common_misconceptions: Data parallelism and model parallelism cannot be combined (they are often used together for very large models)
Doubling the number of GPUs halves training time (communication overhead limits linear scaling)

name: SageMaker Endpoints
description: Deploying trained models as managed real-time inference endpoints with auto-scaling, A/B testing, and various deployment strategies
key_facts: Real-time endpoints provide synchronous inference with low latency for online predictions
Serverless endpoints automatically scale to zero and provision compute on demand, ideal for intermittent traffic
Auto-scaling policies adjust instance count based on CloudWatch metrics like InvocationsPerInstance
Blue/green deployments enable zero-downtime model updates by shifting traffic between endpoint variants
Shadow testing sends duplicate traffic to a new model variant without affecting production responses
common_misconceptions: All endpoints require provisioned instances running 24/7 (serverless endpoints scale to zero when idle)
You must deploy one model per endpoint (multi-model and multi-container endpoints host multiple models)

name: Multi-Model and Multi-Container Endpoints
description: Hosting multiple models on a single endpoint to reduce costs and simplify management for applications serving many models
key_facts: Multi-model endpoints (MME) host thousands of models on a single endpoint, loading them dynamically from S3
Models are loaded into memory on demand and unloaded when not in use (LRU caching)
Multi-container endpoints run multiple containers (different frameworks/models) behind a single endpoint
MME is ideal for scenarios with many models but low per-model traffic (e.g., per-customer models)
Inference pipelines chain multiple containers for sequential preprocessing and inference
common_misconceptions: Each model needs its own dedicated endpoint (MME can host thousands of models on one endpoint)
Multi-model endpoints have the same latency as single-model endpoints (cold loading from S3 adds latency for infrequently used models)

name: SageMaker Pipelines and Step Functions for ML
description: Orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows using SageMaker Pipelines for model building and Step Functions for broader application integration
key_facts: SageMaker Pipelines defines ML workflows as a DAG with steps for processing, training, evaluation, and deployment
Pipeline steps include Processing, Training, Tuning, Transform, Model, Condition, and Lambda steps
Pipeline parameters allow runtime configuration without changing the pipeline definition
Step Functions provides broader orchestration including non-SageMaker steps (Glue, Lambda, ECS)
SageMaker Model Registry stores model versions with approval statuses for CI/CD deployment
common_misconceptions: SageMaker Pipelines and Step Functions serve the same purpose (Pipelines is ML-specific; Step Functions handles broader application workflows)
Pipelines must run all steps every time (conditional steps can skip stages based on evaluation metrics)

name: Step Functions for ML Workflows
description: Using AWS Step Functions to orchestrate complex ML workflows that span multiple AWS services beyond SageMaker
key_facts: Step Functions has native SageMaker integrations for training, transform, and endpoint operations
Supports error handling with retry and catch blocks for resilient ML pipelines
Parallel state enables concurrent execution of multiple model training jobs
Map state iterates over a dataset to process items in parallel (e.g., batch processing)
Integrates with 200+ AWS services for end-to-end automation including Glue, Lambda, and SNS
common_misconceptions: Step Functions is only for simple linear workflows (it supports complex branching, parallelism, and iteration)
Step Functions replaces SageMaker Pipelines (they complement each other for different orchestration needs)

name: SageMaker Model Monitor
description: Continuously monitoring deployed ML models for data quality, model quality, bias drift, and feature attribution drift
key_facts: Data Quality monitoring detects deviations in input data statistics (missing values, type changes, distribution shifts)
Model Quality monitoring tracks prediction accuracy metrics against ground truth labels
Bias Drift monitoring detects changes in bias metrics over time using SageMaker Clarify
Feature Attribution Drift monitoring detects changes in feature importance relative to a baseline
Monitoring schedules run on a configurable cadence (hourly, daily) and generate violation reports
common_misconceptions: Model Monitor only checks data quality (it monitors four aspects: data quality, model quality, bias, and feature attribution)
Model Monitor automatically retrains the model when drift is detected (it alerts; retraining must be triggered separately)

name: Data Drift Detection
description: Identifying when the statistical properties of production data shift from the training data distribution, causing model performance degradation
key_facts: Data drift occurs when the distribution of input features changes over time compared to training data
Concept drift occurs when the relationship between inputs and outputs changes (the target concept evolves)
Statistical tests (KS test, chi-squared test) quantify the difference between training and production distributions
Baselines are created from training data statistics and used as reference for drift detection
CloudWatch alarms can trigger automated retraining pipelines when drift exceeds configurable thresholds
common_misconceptions: Data drift and concept drift are the same thing (data drift is about input distribution changes; concept drift is about the target relationship changing)
A model that performed well during testing will maintain performance indefinitely (all models degrade over time as data distributions shift)

name: SageMaker Security and Access Controls
description: Securing ML workloads on SageMaker using IAM roles, VPC configurations, encryption, and network isolation
key_facts: SageMaker execution roles define IAM permissions for training jobs, endpoints, and notebooks
VPC mode runs training jobs and endpoints within a private VPC with no internet access
Inter-container traffic encryption protects data during distributed training
S3 bucket policies and KMS keys control access to training data and model artifacts
SageMaker notebooks support network isolation and can be restricted to VPC-only access
common_misconceptions: SageMaker workloads automatically run in a VPC (by default they run in a SageMaker-managed network; VPC configuration is optional)
KMS encryption is enabled by default for all SageMaker resources (encryption at rest must be explicitly configured with KMS for some resources)
